abstract: Ferroelectric (FE) semiconductors, such as BaTiO₃, possess a remnant polarization that facilitates the separation of photogenerated charge carriers. In this work, we demonstrate for the first time that BaTiO₃ nanocrystals can achieve significantly enhanced photocatalytic hydrogen evolution and photoelectrochemical water oxidation via this ferroelectric effect. Tetragonal-phase nanocrystals were synthesized hydrothermally from TiO₂ and barium hydroxide with a 63% yield. When deposited as films on tantalum substrates, these nanocrystals exhibited water oxidation photocurrents of 0.141 mA cm⁻² at 1.23 V vs. RHE under UV illumination (60 mW cm⁻²). Systematic electric polarization at 52.8 kV cm⁻¹ normal to the film plane resulted in a twofold increase or a 3.5-fold decrease in photocurrent, depending on the field polarity. This polarization also shifted the onset potential by –0.15 V or +0.09 V and modified the surface photovoltage response. Furthermore, field exposure enhanced the H₂ evolution rate of Pt/BaTiO₃ by approximately 1.5 times and improved the selectivity of silver photodeposition onto the (001) facets. Notably, all ferroelectric enhancements were reversible and could be neutralized by heating the samples above the Curie temperature. These phenomena are attributed to dipole-induced modifications of the potential drop across the space charge layer. Leveraging the ferroelectric effect to tune catalytic activity offers a promising strategy for advancing solar-to-fuel conversion technologies.
